story:
We first saw the footprints on an icy February night. Milly, our black Lab turning gray, three legs and still stubborn, was pawing at a fresh bootprint by the locked cellar hatch. An inch of new snow lay over the frozen ground. A single line of prints came out of the woods and stopped at the basement doors. No prints going back. Something had happened here.

We dragged Milly inside. The cold followed us. I warmed my hands over the last embers in the fireplace and told Heather. She shivered, tried for calm. Probably a kid cutting through, she said. Probably the other tracks had been buried by the snow. I bit back the thought that if that were true, the tracks that had been there longest should have vanished first, not the ones that would have been made later.

This house in the woods had been her Uncle George’s—born outdoors, happiest under stars, fishing, hunting, watching the night. He’d died in mid-February, like now. A tractor trailer with bad brakes slid on ice and broadsided him. He died instantly. Milly, in the back seat, lost a leg. They’d been headed to look at Canis Major, the Great Dog, crossing the meridian this time of year. George had loved Heather—she was the image of his sister, her mother, gone in childbirth—and he left us the house and Milly.

We opened the inside basement door and went down the cold concrete steps into the damp, because the prints ended at the hatch and there was nowhere else for the mystery to begin. A bare bulb, a bad smell, the kind of place we joked could grow mushrooms. An old freezer stood guard. Fishing rods, tackle, camo, a box of shells. Against the wall, his bent refractor on a wobbly tripod, tokyo 1970’s dust and dents. We’d meant to get rid of it all someday. The outside hatch showed nothing but cobwebs, rust, neglect—no sign it had been opened, no scrape in the rust, no fresh break in the grime. Not fear exactly as we stood there. More like we’d brushed against something.

“Where’s Milly?” Heather asked. The dog wouldn’t try stairs now—three legs down is a bad angle for any creature. And she couldn’t have slipped past us without noise. We went up.

There, dark and wet with sleet, a new set of bootprints tracked from the side door along the hall and stopped at Milly’s bed. No sound from the stairs while we were below, no shadow across the open door. And Milly was gone—but the snow was coming down harder now, already beginning to fill any tracks that might have led outside. A one-way walk from nowhere.

Heather stared at the empty bed, at the kitchen calendar, and I saw the moment she understood. “Flashlight,” she said. We went back down. She dug through George’s things and found them under a rain slicker: his old duck boots. The soles matched the prints exactly. She smiled at nothing and everything, not because it made sense, but because for the first time it did. “Jacket. Outside.”

The first tracks had filled in with new snow. And now, barely visible in the fresh powder but growing clearer as we looked, there was a trail from our side door into the trees. Bootprints, and beside them a line of dog paws that started as three and, a few feet from the woods, became a clean set of four, as if the missing leg had simply returned to the work of walking. Both sets walking together, steady, like they were keeping time with someone who knew the way.

The snow had stopped. Sirius burned bright and Canis Major gleamed overhead. The air felt charged and somehow warm. We went back in. The house was suddenly toasty. In the den, a roaring fire we hadn’t built. The logs cracked and leaped. I thought about what little I knew of fixing old telescopes. Heather thought about the unopened bag of dog food and what time the animal shelter opened, because grief makes room and then asks what, if anything, can live in it next. The flames hissed and spat and, just for a second, shaped themselves into a smile.
